---
title: "First-order syntax and substitution"
---

::: {#def-fol-signature .definition name="First-order signature" date="2026-07-12" export="def-fol-signature" status="verified"}
A first-order signature \(L\) consists of sets \(\mathcal F_n\) of \(n\)-ary
function symbols and sets \(\mathcal R_n\) of \(n\)-ary relation symbols for
each \(n\in\mathbb N\).  Constants are the members of \(\mathcal F_0\).
Equality is a distinguished logical binary predicate, not a member of
\(\mathcal R_2\).  A language extension keeps the old symbols with their
arities and adds new ones.
:::

::: {#def-fol-terms .definition name="Terms and variables" date="2026-07-12" export="def-fol-terms" status="verified"}
Fix a countably infinite set \(\mathrm{Var}\) of variables.  For a signature
\(L\) as in @def-fol-signature, the \(L\)-terms are the least set containing
every variable and every constant and closed under
\(f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\) for \(f\in\mathcal F_n\).  A term is *closed* when it
has no variable.  The finite set of variables occurring in \(t\) is
\(\mathrm{Var}(t)\).
:::

::: {#def-fol-formulas .definition name="Formulas and sentences" date="2026-07-12" export="def-fol-formulas" status="verified"}
For @def-fol-signature and @def-fol-terms, the \(L\)-formulas are the finite
syntax trees generated inductively by two clauses: the atomic formulas are
\(R(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\) with \(R\in\mathcal R_n\) and each \(t_i\) an \(L\)-term,
together with \(t=u\) for \(L\)-terms \(t,u\); and from formulas \(A,B\) and a
variable \(x\) one forms \(\neg A\), \((A\wedge B)\), and \(\exists x\,A\).
Formulas are these concrete trees, taken literally and not up to any renaming
of bound variables.  Introduce the remaining connectives as abbreviations for
specific primitive formulas: \(A\vee B:=\neg(\neg A\wedge\neg B)\),
\(A\to B:=\neg(A\wedge\neg B)\),
\(A\leftrightarrow B:=(A\to B)\wedge(B\to A)\), and
\(\forall x\,A:=\neg\exists x\,\neg A\); each abbreviation denotes exactly the
displayed primitive syntax tree.  The set of free variables is defined by
recursion: \(\mathrm{FV}\) of an atomic formula is
the set of variables occurring in its terms; \(\mathrm{FV}(\neg A)=\mathrm{FV}(A)\);
\(\mathrm{FV}(A\wedge B)=\mathrm{FV}(A)\cup\mathrm{FV}(B)\); and
\(\mathrm{FV}(\exists x\,A)=\mathrm{FV}(A)\setminus\{x\}\).  An occurrence of a
variable in a formula is *bound* when it lies inside a subformula \(\exists x\)
binding that variable, and *free* otherwise, so \(\mathrm{FV}(A)\) is exactly
the set of variables with a free occurrence.  A *sentence* is a formula \(A\)
with \(\mathrm{FV}(A)=\varnothing\).
:::

::: {#def-fol-substitution .definition name="Capture-free substitution" date="2026-07-12" export="def-fol-substitution" status="verified"}
For @def-fol-terms and @def-fol-formulas, term substitution \(t[u/x]\) replaces
every occurrence of the variable \(x\) in the term \(t\) by the term \(u\), by
recursion on \(t\): \(x[u/x]=u\); \(y[u/x]=y\) for a variable \(y\ne x\);
\(c[u/x]=c\) for a constant \(c\); and
\(f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)[u/x]=f(t_1[u/x],\ldots,t_n[u/x])\).  A term \(u\) is *free
for* \(x\) in a formula \(A\) when no free occurrence of \(x\) in \(A\) lies
within the scope of a binder \(\exists y\) with \(y\in\mathrm{Var}(u)\).  When
\(u\) is free for \(x\) in \(A\), the formula \(A[u/x]\) is defined by recursion
and replaces the free occurrences of \(x\) by \(u\): on an atomic formula it
substitutes in the terms; \((\neg A)[u/x]=\neg(A[u/x])\);
\((A\wedge B)[u/x]=(A[u/x]\wedge B[u/x])\); and, for the quantifier,
\((\exists y\,A)[u/x]=\exists y\,A\) when \(y=x\), while for \(y\ne x\) — where
the free-for hypothesis forces \(y\notin\mathrm{Var}(u)\) whenever
\(x\in\mathrm{FV}(A)\) — one sets \((\exists y\,A)[u/x]=\exists y\,(A[u/x])\).
Simultaneous substitution \(A[u_1/x_1,\ldots,u_k/x_k]\), with the \(x_i\)
distinct and each \(u_i\) free for \(x_i\) in \(A\), replaces the free
occurrences of every \(x_i\) by \(u_i\) at once under the same clauses.  Because
a closed term has no variables, it is free for every variable in every formula
and its substitution never renames a binder.
:::

::: {#lem-fresh-variable .lemma name="Fresh-variable lemma" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-fresh-variable"}
For every finite collection of terms and formulas there is a variable that
does not occur in any member of the collection.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-fresh-variable" status="verified"}
By @def-fol-terms every term is a finite tree and so contains finitely many
variables; by @def-fol-formulas every formula is a finite syntax tree, so the
set of variables occurring in it, whether bound or free, is finite.  A finite
collection therefore determines a finite union of finite variable sets, which
is finite, whereas \(\mathrm{Var}\) is infinite by @def-fol-terms.  The
complement of that finite union is nonempty, and any variable in it occurs in
no member of the collection.
:::

::: {#lem-substitution-composition .lemma name="Composition of closed substitutions" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-substitution-composition"}
Let \(A\) be a formula, let \(x_1,\ldots,x_k\) be distinct variables, and let
\(r_1,\ldots,r_k\) be closed terms.  Then the simultaneous substitution
\(A[r_1/x_1,\ldots,r_k/x_k]\) of @def-fol-substitution replaces exactly the free
occurrences of each \(x_i\) by \(r_i\) with no renaming, and equals the result
of performing the one-variable substitutions in any order.  Consequently, for a
further closed term \(r\) and a variable \(x\notin\{x_1,\ldots,x_k\}\),
\[
 A[r/x,\,r_1/x_1,\ldots,r_k/x_k]
   =\bigl(A[r_1/x_1,\ldots,r_k/x_k]\bigr)[r/x].
\]
The analogous identity holds for terms.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-substitution-composition" status="verified"}
By @def-fol-terms the terms \(r,r_1,\ldots,r_k\) are closed, hence contain no
variables, so by @def-fol-substitution each is free for every variable in every
formula, every displayed substitution is defined, and none of them renames a
binder or alters which occurrences are bound.  Induct on \(A\) using
@def-fol-formulas.  For a term, and hence for an atomic formula, the identities
are proved by recursion on term structure: distinct variables are replaced
independently, closedness of the \(r_i\) makes the order of replacement
irrelevant, and \(x\notin\{x_1,\ldots,x_k\}\) makes the two sides coincide.  The
cases \(\neg A\) and \(A\wedge B\) follow by applying the induction hypothesis
to the immediate subformulas.  For \(\exists y\,A\): if \(y\) equals some
\(x_i\) or equals \(x\), that variable has no free occurrence in
\(\exists y\,A\), so every substitution simply omits it and both sides discard
it identically; otherwise each displayed substitution passes under the binder
unchanged by @def-fol-substitution, and the induction hypothesis applies to
\(A\).  In every case the simultaneous substitution agrees with the iterated
one, and the displayed identity holds because \(x\) differs from each \(x_i\)
and all substituted terms are closed.
:::

::: {#def-language-translation .definition name="Constant-to-variable translation" date="2026-07-12" export="def-language-translation" status="verified"}
For @def-fol-terms and @def-fol-formulas, let \(c_1,\ldots,c_n\) be distinct
constants and \(y_1,\ldots,y_n\) distinct variables.  The *translation*
replacing each \(c_i\) by \(y_i\) is defined by recursion on syntax: on a term
it replaces every occurrence of the constant symbol \(c_i\) by the variable
\(y_i\), fixes every variable and every other function symbol, and sends
\(f(t_1,\ldots,t_m)\) to \(f\) applied to the translated arguments; on formulas
it acts inside atomic formulas through their terms and commutes with \(\neg\),
\(\wedge\), and every binder \(\exists x\) left unchanged, since a binder binds
a variable while the translation alters only constant symbols.  It fixes every
term and formula that contains none of the \(c_i\).  Because it changes no
binder, a variable other than \(y_1,\ldots,y_n\) has a free occurrence in the
translated object exactly where it had one in the original.  If moreover none of
the \(y_i\) occurs in the object being translated, then no binder \(\exists y_i\)
is present, so each occurrence of the constant \(c_i\) becomes a free occurrence
of the variable \(y_i\).
:::
